coredata.dc:title: A dual quantum dots-based ratio fluorescent probe for detecting Glyphosate in agricultural products

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Gly in real samples
Working Principle of the bCDs/rQDs ratio fluorescence probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of bCDs (A) and rQDs (B), respectively. Insets from left to right show images of the samples under daylight and UV light. UV–Vis and fluorescence (FL) spectra of bCDs (C) and rQDs (D), respectively.
FL spectra of bCDs (A) and rQDs (B) under different excitation wavelengths, respectively. (C) FL spectra of the bCDs/rQDs probe under different excitation wavelengths.

(A) Fluorescence lifetime spectra of rQDs and rQDs + Gly. (B) UV–Vis spectrum of Gly and FL spectrum of rQDs. (C) FL spectra of rQDs with the addition of different concentrations of Gly. (D) Fluorescence image of rQDs solutions with the addition of different concentrations of Gly under a 365 nm UV lamp (1:rQDs, 2: rQDs + 5 μM Gly, 3: rQDs + 50 μM Gly, 4: rQDs + 250 μM Gly, 5: rQDs + 500 μM Gly).
(A) FL spectra of bCDs, rQDs, and rQDs + Gly. FL spectra of bCDs (B) and rQDs (C) with the addition of different concentrations of Gly, respectively.
(A) The probe response to Gly (50 μM) under different reaction times, (B) with bCDs/rQDs at different ratios, (C) under different pH conditions. (n = 3).

(A) FL changes of the bCDs/rQDs probe after the addition of different concentrations of Gly, (B) Linear fitting curve of the relative fluorescence intensity (I 440/640)/(I 440/640)0 of the bCDs/rQDs probe versus CGly (n = 3). (C) Images of the bCDs/rQDs probe after the addition of different concentrations of Gly under UV light.

Selectivity of the ratio fluorescent sensing platform for the target substance Gly and interfering ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cl-, SO4 2
- , H2PO4 -), interfering pesticides (acephate, trichlorfon, dimethoate), and other sugars and amino acids (L-histidine, reduced glutathione, D-glucose), with all substances at a concentration of 10 μM. (n = 3).
Schematic illustration of ratiometric fluorescent sensing of Gly using a nanoprobe based on bCDs and rQDs.
Comparison of the probe performance of this work with those previously reported sensing methods.
The intra-day and inter-day precision and accuracy results of Gly determination（n = 3）.
Determination of Gly content in actual samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A dual quantum dots-based ratio fluorescent probe for detecting Glyphosate in agricultural products

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of New Drug Research and Clinical Pharmacy, Xuzhou Medical University, Xuzhou, Jiangsu 221004, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory of New Drug Research and Clinical Pharmacy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, The Affiliated Jiangyin Clinical College of Xuzhou Medical University, Wuxi 214400, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Affiliated Jiangyin Clinical College of Xuzhou Medical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacy, The Affiliated Jiangyin Clinical College of Xuzhou Medical University, Wuxi, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Children’s Hospital, Zhejiang University School of Medicine, National Clinical Research Center for Child Health, Hangzhou, Zhejiang 310052, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Clinical Research Center for Child Health

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Children’s Hospital, Zhejiang University School of Medicine, National Clinical Research Center for Child Health, Hangzhou, Zhejiang 310052, PR China

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glyphosate (Gly) is one of the most widely used herbicides globally [1]. Due to its broad-spectrum herbicidal activity, high efficiency, and low cost, Gly is extensively used in agricultural fields, orchards, along roads, dikes, and in aquatic environments for weed control [2]. However, as a result of its long-term large-scale use, Gly has inevitably permeated surrounding ecosystems, leading to pollution of soil, water sources, and crops [3]. Since 2015, with the deepening research into the potential health risks of Gly, the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) has classified it as a possibly carcinogenic substance to humans [4]. It has been confirmed that Gly is closely associated with various human diseases and pathological conditions. Long-term exposure to Gly may result in adverse effects, including pregnancy complications in pregnant women [5,6], kidney damage [7], liver inflammation and metabolic syndrome [8], skin diseases and respiratory issues [9], as well as tumors [10]. Therefore, it is essential to conduct quantitative monitoring of Gly in the environment to avoid unnecessary exposure.

The traditional methods for detecting Gly currently include High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) [11], Gas Chromatography (GC) [12], Gas Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (GC–MS) [13], Ion Chromatography (IC) [14], Capillary Electrophoresis (CE) [15], and Enzyme-Linked Immunosorbent Assay (ELISA) [16]. These methods possess high sensitivity and accuracy. However, they generally face challenges such as expensive equipment, complex operation, tedious sample pretreatment, and high detection costs, which greatly limit their application in the analysis of actual samples. Fluorescence analysis is considered a highly promising technique for detecting organophosphate pesticides (Ops) due to its advantages of high sensitivity, fast response, simple operation, and low cost [17]. Therefore, developing a novel, rapid, and economical fluorescent probe for the detection of Gly is of significant importance.

In the existing reports, most fluorescent probes rely solely on a single detection signal to transmit the target substance [18–20]. This makes the analytical performance of the probes susceptible to non-target-related experimental factors, such as probe concentration and inherent stability. To overcome this limitation, ratio fluorescent probes have emerged. These probes utilize the ratio of two or more emission bands at different wavelengths as analytical signals, providing an intrinsic self-calibration function through the ratio of the two fluorescence signals as output. This design not only delivers multiple pieces of information, reducing or even eliminating interference from external factors but also offsets the errors associated with a single emission signal, resulting in higher accuracy and reliability in detection results [21]. With these advantages, ratiometric fluorescent probes have been widely applied in chemical sensing [22] and biosensing technologies [23], further promoting advancements in medical diagnostics [24], drug monitoring [25], food safety testing [26], and environmental monitoring [27]. For instance, researchers have assembled a ratiometric fluorescent probe by encapsulating CdTe quantum dots (QDs) and Rhodamine 6G into magnetic mesoporous silica nanocomposites, which enhanced the sensitivity of Hg2+ detection and enabled good reusability of the probe through magnetic separation [28]. However, the synthesis of multifunctional nanocomposites is complex and laborious, limiting the applicability of this analytical method. Yang et al. [29] developed a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on boron nitride quantum dot and europium ion (BNQD-Eu3+) for tetracycline detection. By integrating colorimetric and fluorescence methods, this approach improved the accuracy of tetracycline detection and enabled the visual detection of tetracycline residues in milk and beef by developing test paper. However, the prolonged synthesis process of the BNQD material restricts its application in real sample analysis. Liu et al. [30] designed a ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe using a rapidly and directly prepared, unmodified dual-emission carbon dot system for trace nitrite determination. This probe exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity, yet it was susceptible to environmental temperature variations, necessitating a stable temperature during measurements, which limited its practical applications. Therefore, by drawing on the advantages of these probes while overcoming the drawbacks of complex and time-consuming synthesis and poor stability, the development of a rapidly preparable and highly stable ratiometric fluorescent probe for Gly detection will significantly enhance the speed and reliability of the detection process. This approach offers a novel strategy for food safety monitoring and environmental pollutant assessment.

Carbon Dots (CDs) and QDs are novel fluorescent nanomaterials that have garnered widespread application due to their high quantum yield, strong resistance to photobleaching, and larger Stokes shifts compared to traditional organic fluorophores [20]. These exceptional optical properties make them critical components in the development of fluorescence-based analytical methods. QDs and CDs can serve as direct probes for target analytes or be combined with other fluorescent materials to form ratiometric fluorescence probes [31]. These ratiometric probes leverage the advantages of QDs and CDs, which emit at different wavelengths, to detect targets through direct or indirect interactions. Typically, the fluorescence quenching mechanisms associated with QDs and CDs include Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET), Photoinduced Electron Transfer (PET), Inner Filter Effect (IFE), dynamic quenching, and static quenching [32]. Huang et al. used a hydrogel embedded with CDs and CdTe QDs as an indicator for Cu2+ detection [33]. The presence of Cu2+ quenched the red fluorescence of CdTe QDs, while the blue fluorescence of CDs remained unaffected. This occurs as Cu2+ adsorbs onto the surface of CdTe QDs via 3-mercaptopropionic acid, triggering electron transfer that reduces Cu2+ to Cu+. This promotes non-radiative recombination of excited electrons with holes in the valence band, leading to effective quenching the fluorescence of CdTe QDs. Chu et al. developed a dual-emission wavelength probe composed of Ce-doped CdTe QDs and CDs to directly interact with Hg2+ [34]. Hg2+ significantly quenched the red fluorescence of CdTe QDs while having little effect on the fluorescence of CDs. The low solubility product (Ksp) of the Hg-S bond compared to the Cd-S bond allows CdTe QDs to preferentially adsorb Hg2+, transforming part of CdS into HgS, which leads to the formation of non-fluorescent substances and reduced fluorescence intensity. This quenching mechanism is analogous to how metal ions quench the fluorescence of apple condensed tannins (ACT) through a static quenching mechanism, where ACT interacts with metal ions primarily via ion exchange with catechol groups [35]. The ability of nanofluorescent materials to directly interact with target analytes provides a simple and rapid detection method, showcasing strong analytical capabilities and broad potential applications.

In this study, a ratiometric fluorescence probe was developed based on blue carbon dots (bCDs) and red quantum dots (rQDs) for the quantitative detection of Gly. Upon the addition of Gly to the system, the fluorescence of rQDs is rapidly quenched, serving as the detection signal for Gly, while the fluorescence of bCDs remains unchanged and acts as the reference signal. The quantitative detection of Gly is achieved by monitoring the changes in the fluorescence intensity ratio of the bCDs/rQDs ratiometric fluorescence probe. Finally, the prepared ratiometric fluorescence probe was applied to detect the residual Gly content in real apple samples.

Anhydrous ethanol (C2H5OH), acetone (CH3COCH3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), hydrochloric acid (HCl), quinine sulfate dihydrate, magnesium sulfate anhydrous (Mg2SO4), sodium chloride (NaCl), and potassium chloride (KCl) were purchased from China National Pharmaceutical Group Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Diethylenetriamine (DETA, 97 %), monohydrate citric acid (CA, 99.5 %), dimethoate standard solution, calcium chloride anhydrous (CaCl2), and reduced glutathione were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Glyphosate (Gly, 95 %), D-anhydrous glucose (98 %), acephate standard solution, potassium phosphate monobasic (99.5 %), trichlorphon standard solution, and sodium ion standard solution were sourced from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. L-Histidine was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai) Trading Co., Ltd. Water-soluble CdSe/ZnS Quantum Dots (640 nm) were obtained from Xingzi New Materials Technology Development Co., Ltd. All chemicals were acquired from commercial suppliers and were used without further purification. All purified water was prepared using an ultrapure water system (18 MΩ·cm) from Thermo Scientific, China.

Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectra were measured using a UV-2600 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were collected with an F4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), with both excitation and emission slit widths set to 5 nm. A transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan) was used to characterize the morphological features. Other instruments used in this experiment included an ultrasonic cleaner (Kunshan Ultrasonic Instruments, China), ME104 electronic balance (Mettler, Switzerland), 78–1 magnetic heating stirrer (Suzhou Weier, China), vacuum drying oven (Shanghai Yiheng, China), constant temperature mixer (Shanghai Jingxin, China), Thermo Scientific ultrapure water system (Thermo Fisher Scientific, China), H1850 high-speed centrifuge (Hunan Xiangyi, China), and a Vortex QL-902 multifunctional vortex mixer (Haimen Qilinbel, China).

bCDs were synthesized based on a reported method with minor modifications [36]. Specifically, 1.2 g of citric acid (CA) and 600 μL of diethylenetriamine (DETA) were mixed with 20 mL of ultrapure water and sonicated for 20 min to facilitate dissolution, followed by uniform mixing on a shaker. The solution was then transferred into a stainless steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and reacted at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was washed with a mixture of ethanol and acetone, which resulted in the precipitation of yellow particles. The supernatant was removed by centrifugation, and the resulting solid was dried in a vacuum drying oven at 50 °C for 48 h. Finally, the obtained yellow solid powder was identified as bCDs and stored at room temperature.

First, mix 100 μL of rQDs and 100 μL of bCDs thoroughly, achieving final concentrations of 26 μg/mL for rQDs and 0.325 μg/mL for bCDs. Adjust the pH of the solution to 6.0 using 0.1 M hydrochloric acid to construct the bCDs/rQDs ratio fluorescence probe. Then, mix 200 μL of the bCDs/rQDs ratio fluorescence probe solution with 50 μL of different concentrations of Gly (final concentrations of Gly are 0, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 50, 100, and 200 μM) and let it react at room temperature for 30 min. Measure the fluorescence spectrum of the mixture and plot the standard curve of I 440/I 640 against the Gly concentration. The excitation wavelength for all fluorescence measurements is set at 345 nm, with an emission slit width of 5 nm and a voltage of 850 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Gly in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Residues of Gly in the environment are usually ingested by humans through water, fruits, and vegetables. The actual samples in this study consisted of potatoes, gingers, and apples purchased from a market near Xuzhou Medical University. First, two slices of potato, minimizing the inclusion of flesh, were cut from the potato. Then, the potato peel was placed in a beaker containing 20 mL of ultrapure water and subjected to ultrasonic treatment for 12 min. The mixture was then filtered through a 0.22 µm microporous membrane. Finally, the filtrate was diluted five times and stored at room temperature for future use. The samples did not require any additional treatment to evaluate the applicability of the fluorescence sensor in real samples. To simulate pesticide contamination, this study used the standard addition method to determine the residue levels of Gly. Gingers and apples are detected by the same method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working Principle of the bCDs/rQDs ratio fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: This study developed a ratio fluorescence probe based on bCDs and rQDs for the detection of Gly, successfully applied to the detection of Gly in agricultural products. As shown in the schematic diagram (Scheme 1 ), the prepared bCDs are mixed with rQDs at a specific concentration ratio at room temperature to form the bCDs/rQDs ratio fluorescence probe. This probe exhibits two fluorescence emission peaks under excitation at a single wavelength of 345 nm, which are located at 440 nm and 640 nm, respectively. Upon the addition of the target molecule Gly, the fluorescence of rQDs (at 640 nm) is quenched, while the fluorescence of bCDs (at 440 nm) remains stable. As the concentration of Gly increases, the fluorescence intensity of rQDs continues to weaken, whereas the fluorescence intensity of bCDs remains relatively constant. Therefore, using the fluorescence intensity of bCDs as a reference and the fluorescence intensity of rQDs as the detection signal, quantitative detection of Gly can be achieved based on the changes in the fluorescence intensity ratio (I 440/I 640).

As shown in the TEM image of bCDs in Fig. 1 A, the bCDs exhibit a spherical shape with uniform dispersion and an average particle size of approximately 3.8 nm. The inset in Fig. 1A shows that bCDs form a colorless, transparent solution under daylight and exhibit strong blue fluorescence under UV light. Fig. 1B displays the TEM image of rQDs, which are nearly spherical with uniform dispersion and a particle size of about 2.3 nm. The inset in Fig. 1B illustrates that, similar to bCDs, rQDs also appear as a colorless, transparent solution under daylight, but emit intense red fluorescence under UV light. Additionally, both bCDs and rQDs were characterized using UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 1C shows the UV–Vis spectra, where bCDs exhibit prominent absorption peaks at 240 nm and 352 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition of C=C aromatic rings and the n-π* transition of C=O, respectively. In contrast, rQDs show broad absorption across the 230–800 nm range without any distinct absorption peaks. Upon excitation at 345 nm, the maximum fluorescence emission wavelengths are observed at 440 nm for bCDs and 640 nm for rQDs, as shown in Fig. 1D. Comparison with UV and fluorescence spectra in the literature confirms the successful synthesis of both bCDs and rQDs [37,38]. In addition, the quantum yield (QY) of the fluorescent materials was determined using a relative measurement method [39,40], with the QY values of bCDs and rQDs measured at 31.0 % and 10.9 %, respectively. These results further demonstrate that bCDs and rQDs possess excellent fluorescence properties, making them suitable for constructing ratiometric fluorescent sensing platforms.

Subsequently, to achieve optimal fluorescence performance, the emission spectra of the nanomaterials were measured under different excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the fluorescence intensity of bCDs reached its maximum under excitation at 345 nm and 355 nm. In Fig. 2B, the fluorescence intensity of rQDs under 325 nm and 335 nm excitation was significantly affected by harmonic peaks, making it difficult to distinguish or even leading to complete overlap. However, at an excitation wavelength of 345 nm, rQDs exhibited stronger fluorescence emission with minimal interference from harmonic peaks, allowing for accurate detection and peak resolution. Further investigation was carried out to explore the emission peak behavior of the bCDs/rQDs probe under different excitation conditions. As shown in Fig. 2C, the bCDs/rQDs probe achieved the best peak resolution and optimal fluorescence response performance when excited at 345 nm. Therefore, 345 nm was chosen as the excitation wavelength for the bCDs/rQDs probe.

As shown in the Scheme 1, the fluorescence of rQDs is quenched upon the addition of Gly to the system. To investigate the quenching mechanism of rQDs fluorescence, further studies were conducted. As shown in Fig. 3 A the fluorescence lifetime of rQDs decreased from 8.5 ns to 4.1 ns upon the addition of Gly, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of rQDs may be caused by charge or energy transfer during dynamic quenching [41]. Fig. 3B shows that there is no significant overlap between the fluorescence emission spectrum of rQDs and the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of Gly, which rules out FRET as a potential quenching mechanism. This is because FRET typically requires sufficient overlap between the emission spectrum of the donor and the absorption spectrum of the acceptor for effective energy transfer [42].

As shown in Fig. 3C, the fluorescence intensity of rQDs decreases as the concentration of Gly increases, accompanied by a red shift in the emission wavelength. This observation excludes photoinduced electron transfer (PET) and intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) mechanisms as the possible quenching mechanism, as PET-induced quenching typically does not cause a spectral shift. Instead, ICT mechanism can explain these phenomena [43]. The –NH2 of Gly interact with the –COOH on the surface of rQDs, where the electron-donating effect of –NH2 enhances the electron-withdrawing properties of –COOH on the rQDs. This interaction promotes the tendency of excited-state electrons in rQDs to transition over an intermediate state, reducing the energy gap and causing the observed red shift in the emission spectrum [44]. As illustrated in Fig. 3D, the rQDs gradually aggregate and precipitate with increasing Gly concentrations, suggesting an interaction that alters the surface charge state of rQDs, affecting the stability of the solution. Thus, the fluorescence quenching of rQDs can be attributed to ICT.

To verify the feasibility of the experimental design, the fluorescent interaction between bCDs and rQDs was explored. As shown in Fig. 4 A, there is no fluorescence interference between bCDs and rQDs. Fig. 4B and 4C illustrate the effect of different concentrations of Gly on the fluorescence intensity of bCDs and rQDs. The fluorescence of bCDs remained almost unchanged after the addition of varying concentrations of Gly, while the fluorescence of rQDs was progressively quenched with increasing Gly concentrations, showing a concentration-dependent effect. These results demonstrate that the fluorescence intensity of bCDs is not affected by Gly, making it suitable as an internal reference signal in the ratiometric fluorescent system. Meanwhile, the fluorescence intensity of rQDs is quenched to varying degrees, serving as the detection signal for Gly. By investigating the interactions between key components in the probe detection system, it is shown that the selected bCDs and rQDs can be successfully applied to construct a ratiometric fluorescence detection platform, providing a stable and reliable method for the quantitative analysis of Gly.

To achieve optimal performance of the sensing platform, this study selected a reaction concentration of 50 μM of Gly for the optimization of reaction time, the mixing ratio of bCDs/rQDs, and the reaction pH. As illustrated in Fig. 5 A, the relative fluorescence intensity ratio of the system exhibited fluctuations within the first 30 min after the addition of Gly. Subsequently, the ratio gradually increased over time, reaching its maximum value at 30 min. This phenomenon can be attributed to the interaction between the sensor and Gly occurring during this period. Thereafter, the relative fluorescence intensity ratio stabilized, indicating that the reaction was complete at 30 min. Therefore, 30 min was chosen as the optimal reaction time for the bCDs/rQDs ratio fluorescent system.

To further enhance detection results, the mixing ratio of bCDs and rQDs was also optimized. As shown in Fig. 5B, as the mixing ratio of rQDs to bCDs increased, the fluorescence intensity ratio correspondingly rose, reaching a maximum when the mixing concentration ratio of rQDs to bCDs was 80:1, after which an opposite trend was observed. This phenomenon occurred because the red fluorescence of rQDs, serving as the detection signal, was quenched upon the addition of Gly. Increasing the proportion of rQDs concentration facilitates the reaction process until saturation was reached. Consequently, 80:1 was selected as the optimal ratio of rQDs to bCDs.

The ratiometric fluorescent probe bCDs/rQDs used in this study consists of uniformly dispersed, relatively stable particulate materials, without ions as surface modifiers [40] or indirect targets [45] in the sensing process. To minimize interference from external ions, 0.1 M HCl or NaOH was directly used [46], instead of buffer solutions, to adjust the pH of the probe (4.5, 5.0, 5.5, 6.0, 6.5, and 7.0) for the detection of Gly, aiming to determine the optimal pH for the application of the developed probe. As depicted in Fig. 5C, when the pH was adjusted to 6.0, the value of (I 440/640)/(I 440/640)0 reached its maximum. This phenomenon arose from the presence of phosphate, carboxyl, and amino groups in Gly molecules, while the surface of rQDs contained carboxyl groups. Both Gly and rQDs underwent protonation and deprotonation in response to pH variations in the solution [31,45], which affected their interaction and reduced the sensitivity of the sensor. When the pH was below 6.0, the functional groups of Gly and the carboxyl-containing quantum dot surface became protonated, leading to a decreased dipole moment and a weakened interaction between Gly and the rQDs surface [46]. Conversely, when the pH exceeded 6.0, Gly readily underwent neutralization reactions, altering its surface functional groups, which hindered its interaction with rQDs. Therefore, pH 6.0 was determined to be the optimal detection pH.

Under optimal conditions, quantitative analysis was performed using the prepared ratio fluorescent probe. As shown in Fig. 6 A, with increasing Gly concentrations in the system, the fluorescence intensity of bCDs remained constant while that of rQDs gradually decreased. Fig. 6B illustrated a positive correlation between the fluorescence intensity ratio of the probe and Gly concentration (0–200 μM). Further studies confirmed a linear relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity ratio (I 440/640)/(I 440/640)0 and Gly concentration within the range of 0–200 μM, with the linear equation given by (I 440/640)/(I 440/640)0 = 0.01689C + 1.0000, where r = 0.9930 (n = 3, and C is the Gly concentration). The limit of detection (LOD) for Gly was calculated to be 0.11 μM using the formula 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample (n = 10) and k is the slope of the calibration curve. This value is below the maximum contaminant levels (MCL) established by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency and China (0.7 mg L−1 and 4.14 μM) [47]. As shown in Fig. 6C, the red fluorescence intensity of rQDs in the ratiometric probe gradually decreased with increasing Gly concentration. When the Gly concentration reached higher levels (50–200 μM), a noticeable color change occurred in the probe solution, shifting from red to pink to blue. This color transition demonstrated the potential of the developed ratiometric fluorescent probe for achieving visual semi-quantitative detection of Gly through a colorimetric method.

To evaluate the sensitivity of the ratio fluorescent probe for quantitative detection of Gly, we compared the detect linear range and LOD of our probe with those of previously reported Gly detection methods. As shown in Table 1 , the developed ratio fluorescent probe demonstrated significant cost-effectiveness and convenience compared to traditional instrument-based analytical methods. Furthermore, the LOD obtained from our probe exhibited higher or comparable sensitivity compared to other reported analytical methods, meeting the requirements for high sensitivity detection standards. These results indicated that the prepared ratio fluorescent probe has good detection sensitivity for Gly.

The precision and accuracy of the quantitative detection method for Gly were investigated. Given its linear range of 0.5 to 200 μM, three validation concentrations were set at low, medium, and high levels of 10.0, 50.0, and 150.0 μM, respectively, to calculate intra-day and inter-day accuracy and relative standard deviation (RSD). The results are presented in Table 2 . The inter-day and intra-day accuracy for the three validated concentrations ranged from 90.5 % to 107.3 %, meeting the sample testing requirements. Additionally, the intra-day and inter-day RSD values ranged from 2.2 % to 9.7 %, which also conform to the analytical requirements for biological samples. These results indicate that the probe developed in this study is suitable for the determination of Gly content in actual samples.

Considering that actual samples may contain not only the target substance Gly but also other compounds such as pesticides, metal ions, amino acids, and sugars, which could potentially affect the detection results, it is essential to evaluate the selectivity and anti-interference capability of the ratio fluorescent probe before applying it to real sample analysis [54,55]. As shown in Fig. 7 , the probe exhibits a high response to Gly, while showing no significant response to interfering ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Cl−, SO4 2−, H2PO4 −), pesticide residues (acephate, trichlorfon, dimethoate), other sugars, and amino acids (L-histidine, reduced glutathione, D-glucose anhydrous). The (I 440/640)/(I 440/640)0 values for these interfering substances remained between 0.9 and 1.0, which is significantly higher than the detection values for Gly, indicating that the probe exhibits a high degree of specificity towards Gly and demonstrates excellent anti-interference capability.

This study investigated the practicality of the probe by measuring the Gly content in agricultural products such as potato, ginger and apple. Given the complex composition of actual samples, the standard addition method was employed to detect Gly in real samples, thereby minimizing interference from the complex matrix on the probe's measurements [31]. The results, as shown in Table 3 , indicate that the recovery rates of Gly in the potatoes ranged from 96.6 % to 104.9 %, with an RSD range of 1.7 % to 5.3 %. In the gingers, the recovery rates of Gly ranged from 95.5 % to 99.8 %, with an RSD range of 4.9 % to 10.5 %. In the apples, the recovery rates of Gly ranged from 92.8 % to 100.6 %, with an RSD range of 3.9 % to 11.2 %. No residual levels of Gly were detected in the actual samples without added Gly standard solution, indicating that the Gly residue levels in the purchased potatoes, gingers, and apples did not exceed national standards. Both the recovery rate and RSD of this method meet the detection requirements for the samples [56], confirming the accuracy and reliability of this method for analyzing real samples. Furthermore, the recovery and RSD data demonstrate that the probe has significant potential for application in the detection of actual samples.

This study successfully developed a ratio fluorescent probe, bCDs/rQDs, based on bCDs and rQDs for the detection of Gly. The bCDs showed no response to Gly and served as a reference signal, while the fluorescence of rQDs was effectively quenched by Gly, acting as the detection signal. Detection was achieved based on the changes in the fluorescence intensity ratio (I 440/640). The probe exhibited a limit of detection (LOD) for Gly as low as 0.11 μM, surpassing that of most existing Gly detection sensors. It has been successfully applied to detect Gly in agricultural products, such as potatoes，gingers and apples. The probe developed in this study not only enriches the methods available for Gly detection but also provides a novel, efficient, and reliable detection strategy for food safety monitoring, biomedical research, and environmental pollutant assessment.

Qian Xiang and Wei Geng conceived and conducted the experiments, and contributed to the manuscript writing. Xiaoqi Guo conducted the experiments and collected the data. Yongjie Liu, Sunan Liu and Xiaojuan Jin performed the data analysis. Yajuan Chang and Tongze Lin assisted performed elementary experiments. Ling Zhu conceived and designed the experiments. Yunsu Ma oversaw the experimental procedures and contributed to manuscript revision and funding acquisition. All authors reviewed and approved the final manuscript.
This study does not involve human participants or animal subjects.

Qian Xiang: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Wei Geng: Project administration, Methodology. Xiaoqi Guo: Validation, Software, Project administration, Methodology. Yongjie Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Sunan Liu: Methodology. Xiaojuan Jin: Software. Yajuan Chang: Validation. Tongze Lin: Methodology. Ling Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Yunsu Ma: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

This work was financially supported by General Project of Natural Science of Jiangsu Province (BK20221215), the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Project of Xuzhou Medical University Science Park (CXCYYB2022010), and Qianlan Projects of Jiangsu Province.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by General Project of Natural Science of Jiangsu Province (BK20221215), the Innovation and Entrepreneurship Project of Xuzhou Medical University Science Park (CXCYYB2022010), and Qianlan Projects of Jiangsu Province.
